text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY AND BONE MARROW TRANSPLANT
COMPREHENSIVE 1-YEAR POST-TRANSPLANT FOLLOW-UP NOTE

**PATIENT:** Margaret Chen
**DOB:** 1977
**DIAGNOSES:** (1) Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML), NOS, de novo, FLT3-ITD positive (high allelic ratio 0.68), DNMT3A R882H, RUNX1 S291fs, TET2 Q916* - ELN 2022 Adverse Risk; (2) Chronic graft-versus-host disease, skin and GI, mild-moderate, in remission; (3) Hypothyroidism (pre-existing)
**TRANSPLANT TYPE:** Matched unrelated donor (MUD) allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (alloHSCT), 10/10 HLA-matched (A, B, C, DRB1, DQB1), male donor age 32, peripheral blood stem cells, CD34+ dose 5.2 x 10^6/kg
**CONDITIONING:** Myeloablative fludarabine/busulfan (FluBu4), pharmacokinetically dosed
**GVHD PROPHYLAXIS:** Tacrolimus/Trexall
**ATTENDING:** Hematology/Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Chen presents today for her 1-year post-allogeneic stem cell transplant comprehensive evaluation. She reports doing well overall and is notably pleased to have reached this milestone. She is here for routine assessment, review of bone marrow biopsy results, and long-term survivorship planning.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Chen is a 49-year-old female high school biology teacher with a history of de novo AML (FLT3-ITD positive with high allelic ratio of 0.68, along with co-mutations in DNMT3A, RUNX1, and TET2 — ELN 2022 Adverse risk), who presented approximately one year ago with progressive fatigue, gingival bleeding, easy bruising, and a 12-pound unintentional weight loss. At diagnosis, her WBC was 87,400/uL with circulating blasts at approximately 40%, hemoglobin 6.8 g/dL, and platelets 32,000/uL. Bone marrow biopsy at that time demonstrated a hypercellular marrow (95% cellularity) with 72% myeloblasts and Auer rods on smear.

She received standard 7+3 induction (cytarabine 100 mg/m2/day continuous infusion days 1-7 plus daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 days 1-3) in combination with midostaurin 50 mg PO BID on days 8-21 per the RATIFY protocol. Her induction course was complicated by febrile neutropenia with bilateral lower lobe pulmonary consolidations (treated with cefepime and micafungin over a 14-day course), grade 2 mucositis, and midostaurin-associated nausea. She achieved a morphologic complete remission (CR) with MRD negativity by 8-color flow cytometry on day 28 bone marrow biopsy. Given her adverse-risk disease — FLT3-ITD with high allelic ratio in a NPM1-unmutated, normal karyotype setting, compounded by RUNX1 frameshift and DNMT3A mutations — the decision was made to proceed directly to allogeneic SCT without consolidation chemotherapy.

Her sibling was a 7/10 HLA match (not suitable), and a 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated male donor (age 32, CMV seropositive) was identified through the unrelated donor registry. Pre-transplant restaging PET-CT confirmed complete metabolic remission with no FDG-avid disease. She underwent myeloablative FluBu4 conditioning (fludarabine 40 mg/m2/day and pharmacokinetically dosed busulfan 3.2 mg/kg/day, days -6 through -3) with levetiracetam seizure prophylaxis, and received peripheral blood stem cells on Day 0. GVHD prophylaxis was tacrolimus/MTX, with letermovir CMV prophylaxis starting Day +1.

The early post-transplant period was complicated by grade 3 mucositis requiring TPN and IV morphine PCA (resolving by Day +18), transiently supratherapeutic tacrolimus levels with mild acute kidney injury (creatinine peak 1.4 mg/dL, returned to baseline), and no bacteremia or documented invasive fungal infection. Engraftment was confirmed on Day +30 bone marrow biopsy with 98% total donor chimerism, 99% CD33+ myeloid chimerism, and MRD negativity.

She was discharged on Day +28. At the Day +100 visit she developed clinical chronic GVHD: lichenoid erythematous papules on bilateral forearms and dorsal hands (~10-15% BSA), confirmed histologically on skin punch biopsy showing lichenoid tissue reaction with basal layer vacuolization and dyskeratotic keratinocytes (NIH grade 2 skin GVHD). Mild GI involvement with 2-3 loose stools per day and mild cramping was also noted (NIH GI score 1). Overall NIH global cGVHD severity was mild. She was managed with topical clobetasol to skin lesions, oral budesonide 3 mg TID for GI symptoms, and continuation of tacrolimus without escalation to systemic steroids. By Day +180, skin GVHD had markedly improved to <5% BSA involvement, and GI symptoms had partially improved.

At her 6-month bone marrow biopsy (Day +180), she maintained morphologic and molecular CR with 99.5% total donor chimerism, 100% CD33+ myeloid chimerism, and MRD negativity. Today's visit marks her 1-year post-transplant comprehensive evaluation (Day +365).

Since the Day +180 visit, she reports continued gradual improvement in all parameters. Her GI cGVHD fully resolved and budesonide was successfully tapered and discontinued at month 9 post-transplant. Tacrolimus was successfully tapered and discontinued at month 11 (approximately 6 weeks ago) without any GVHD flare. She reports her energy levels have returned substantially to baseline. She has returned to part-time teaching and describes her quality of life as good, noting some residual mild fatigue and occasional xerostomia but otherwise tolerating daily activities well. She denies any recurrence of skin rash, diarrhea, or oral mucosal ulceration. She reports normal appetite and stable weight. No fevers, chills, night sweats, or new lymphadenopathy. No dyspnea, cough, or pleuritic pain. No neurological complaints. Thyroid function has been stable on levothyroxine.

Today's bone marrow biopsy and aspirate were performed and results are reviewed below.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: No fever, no chills, no unintentional weight loss. Mild residual fatigue, improving. No night sweats.
Skin: No new rash, no active papules or plaques. Xerostomia mild and stable.
HEENT: No oral ulcers, no mucosal changes. Mild dry eyes (new over past 2-3 months, being assessed).
Respiratory: No dyspnea on exertion or at rest. No cough. No pleuritic chest pain.
Cardiovascular: No palpitations, no chest pain, no lower extremity edema.
Gastrointestinal: Normal bowel habits, formed stools, no blood per rectum, no abdominal cramping. No nausea or vomiting.
Genitourinary: Normal urinary frequency and color. No dysuria.
Musculoskeletal: No joint pain or swelling. No muscle cramps.
Neurological: No headaches, no focal deficits, no peripheral neuropathy.
Endocrine: Managed hypothyroidism, no new symptoms of hypo- or hyperthyroidism.
Hematologic: No spontaneous bleeding, no bruising, no petechiae.
Psychiatric: Mood good; some anxiety about relapse which is being addressed with patient education and support.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, temperature 98.2 degrees F, O2 saturation 98% on room air, weight 62.4 kg (reported baseline ~63 kg at diagnosis; current weight stable).

General: Well-appearing female in no acute distress. Alert and oriented. Pleasant and engaged.
Skin: No active GVHD papules or plaques on forearms, dorsal hands, or trunk. Mild post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation on bilateral forearms, residual from resolved cGVHD. No lichen planus-like lesions. No new eruptions. No jaundice.
HEENT: Mild conjunctival pallor absent; sclerae anicteric. Oral mucosa moist, intact, without ulceration or lichen-like changes. Mild xerostomia per patient report. No gingival hyperplasia.
Neck: No cervical, supraclavicular, or submandibular lymphadenopathy.
Chest: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rales, or rhonchi.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1, S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Normoactive bowel sounds. Spleen not palpable (compare to 4 cm below left costal margin at presentation). No hepatomegaly on percussion.
Extremities: No peripheral edema. No petechiae or ecchymoses.
Neurological: Grossly intact. No focal motor or sensory deficits.
Lymph Nodes: No peripheral lymphadenopathy detected on examination.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 6.2 k/uL (reference 4.5-11.0)
- ANC: 3,900/uL
- Hemoglobin: 13.1 g/dL (reference 11.5-15.5)
- MCV: 89 fL
- Platelets: 210,000/uL (reference 150-400)
- Reticulocyte count: 1.4% (appropriate)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium 138, Potassium 4.0, Bicarbonate 24, BUN 14, Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL (baseline)
- eGFR: >90 mL/min/1.73 m2
- Glucose 91 mg/dL
- Total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL
- Direct bilirubin 0.2 mg/dL
- ALT 26 U/L (normal)
- AST 22 U/L (normal)
- Alkaline phosphatase 74 U/L (normal)
- Total protein 7.1 g/dL, albumin 4.2 g/dL

Thyroid Function:
- TSH: 2.4 mIU/L (therapeutic, on levothyroxine)

Virology (Infectious Disease Monitoring):
- CMV PCR (peripheral blood): Not detected
- EBV PCR (peripheral blood): Not detected
- HHV-6 PCR: Not detected

Tacrolimus level: Not applicable (tacrolimus discontinued at month 11)

Peripheral Blood Donor Chimerism (STR analysis):
- Total chimerism: 99.8% donor
- CD3+ T cells: 98.5% donor
- CD33+ myeloid cells: 100% donor
- Interpretation: Sustained complete donor chimerism at 1 year

---

IMAGING RESULTS

PET-CT scan of the whole body performed as part of today's 1-year assessment:
No FDG-avid lesions identified in the neck, chest, abdomen, or pelvis. No lymphadenopathy. Spleen measuring 10.2 cm in craniocaudal dimension (normal). Liver normal size and without lesions. Lungs are clear -- the mild right lower lobe ground-glass opacities that were previously noted on the Day +90 CT scan have fully resolved. No bone lesions concerning for extramedullary disease. Overall impression: no evidence of AML recurrence at 1 year post-allogeneic SCT.

---

BONE MARROW BIOPSY RESULTS (Day +365)

Today's bone marrow biopsy and aspirate (right posterior iliac crest) reviewed in detail. Final pathology: normocellular bone marrow with estimated cellularity 40-50%, showing normal trilineage hematopoiesis. Myeloblasts comprise approximately 1% of total nucleated cells (within normal limits). No Auer rods identified. No morphologic evidence of residual or recurrent AML.

Donor chimerism by STR analysis: 99.8% donor (total); CD3+ T cells 98.5% donor; CD33+ myeloid cells 100% donor.

MRD assessment by 10-color multiparameter flow cytometry: negative -- no immunophenotypically aberrant blast population detected above the 0.01% threshold.

Molecular studies:
- FLT3-ITD by quantitative PCR: Not detected (sensitivity 0.001%)
- DNMT3A R882H by digital droplet PCR (ddPCR): Not detected
- RUNX1 S291fs by NGS deep sequencing: Not detected

Cytogenetics (conventional karyotype): 46,XY [20/20 metaphases] -- donor male karyotype confirmed, consistent with complete donor chimerism.

Assessment: Sustained complete molecular remission with full donor chimerism at 12 months post-allogeneic SCT. All index mutations undetectable.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. ACUTE MYELOID LEUKEMIA, FLT3-ITD HIGH ALLELIC RATIO, ADVERSE ELN RISK -- IN SUSTAINED COMPLETE MOLECULAR REMISSION AT 1 YEAR POST-ALLO SCT

This is an excellent result. Ms. Chen had adverse-risk AML with FLT3-ITD (high allelic ratio 0.68), DNMT3A R882H, RUNX1 frameshift, and TET2 nonsense mutations -- a genomic profile associated with significant relapse risk even after intensive therapy. She achieved MRD-negative CR after a single induction cycle of 7+3 plus midostaurin, and underwent a successful 10/10 MUD alloHSCT with FluBu4 myeloablative conditioning. She now presents at the 1-year anniversary with full donor chimerism (99.8% total, 100% CD33+), MRD negativity by flow cytometry and sensitive molecular assays (qPCR for FLT3-ITD, ddPCR for DNMT3A), and clean bone marrow morphology.

These findings are highly reassuring. The graft-versus-leukemia (GvL) effect conferred by her mild cGVHD likely contributes to disease control. Although the first two years post-transplant represent the highest risk period for relapse in FLT3-ITD AML, her current molecular remission status is very encouraging.

- Continue surveillance every 3 months with CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel, and peripheral blood chimerism
- Bone marrow biopsy to be considered at 18 months post-transplant, or sooner if any concerning peripheral blood findings emerge (unexplained cytopenia, loss of chimerism, or reappearance of FLT3-ITD by peripheral blood PCR)
- Patient educated regarding warning signs of relapse: new-onset fatigue, bruising, bleeding, lymphadenopathy, gingival swelling, or unexplained cytopenias -- return to care urgently if these occur
- Post-transplant maintenance therapy with FLT3 inhibitor (e.g., Nexavar or gilteritinib) was considered but not pursued given: (1) MRD-negative status at all post-transplant time points, (2) sustained full donor chimerism, (3) ongoing mild cGVHD providing GvL effect, and (4) absence of high-level evidence for maintenance in this specific scenario at this center's current practice. Will continue monitoring closely; discussion of maintenance can be revisited if any molecular signal emerges.

2. CHRONIC GVHD -- SKIN (LICHENOID) AND GI -- IN COMPLETE REMISSION

Ms. Chen's cGVHD has resolved impressively. Skin cGVHD (lichenoid changes, peak 15% BSA) is in complete clinical remission -- no active papules, plaques, or lichenoid lesions today; residual post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation only. GI cGVHD (loose stools, mild cramping) resolved and budesonide successfully discontinued at month 9. Tacrolimus successfully tapered off at month 11 without GVHD flare. No systemic corticosteroids were ever required.

- No active GVHD-directed therapy required at this time
- Patient instructed to notify us immediately if skin rash, oral ulcers, diarrhea, elevated LFTs, or new sicca symptoms emerge (dry eyes noted -- see below)
- Topical clobetasol no longer needed; can use over-the-counter emollients for any residual skin dryness
- Tacrolimus not being restarted

3. DRY EYES (NEW, POSSIBLE OCULAR cGVHD)

Patient reports new onset of mild dry eyes over the past 2-3 months. In the post-transplant setting, particularly with prior lichenoid skin and GI cGVHD, new sicca symptoms must be evaluated carefully for ocular GVHD, which can silently progress and cause permanent ocular damage if untreated.

- Ophthalmology referral placed today (this also serves the purpose of cataract surveillance given busulfan-containing conditioning regimen)
- Schirmer's test and slit-lamp examination requested
- If ocular GVHD is confirmed, will discuss topical cyclosporine eye drops (Restasis or generic), preservative-free lubricating drops, and/or punctal plugs
- Patient advised to use preservative-free artificial tears liberally in the interim

4. HYPOTHYROIDISM

Pre-existing, managed on levothyroxine. TSH today 2.4 mIU/L -- therapeutic. No adjustments needed. Of note, patients post-allogeneic SCT with busulfan-based conditioning and chronic GVHD are at elevated risk for hypothyroidism and other endocrinopathies as late effects; her pre-existing condition makes monitoring particularly important.

- Continue levothyroxine at current dose
- Annual TSH monitoring (next at 18-month visit)

5. LONG-TERM SURVIVORSHIP AND LATE EFFECTS PLANNING

Ms. Chen has received myeloablative conditioning with busulfan and fludarabine, a 10/10 MUD alloHSCT, and has experienced mild chronic GVHD -- all of which confer specific late-effects risks that require proactive management:

Bone Health:
- Post-transplant patients receiving corticosteroids (even topical) and immunosuppressants are at risk for reduced bone mineral density and avascular necrosis
- DEXA scan ordered at this visit (baseline post-transplant bone density assessment)
- If osteopenia or osteoporosis detected: calcium/vitamin D supplementation, consider bisphosphonate therapy
- Patient encouraged to continue weight-bearing exercise

Cardiovascular:
- Repeat echocardiogram to assess LVEF (baseline pre-treatment LVEF was 62%; busulfan and prior anthracycline exposure); if stable, can transition to every-2-year surveillance
- Lifestyle counseling: healthy diet, regular aerobic exercise, avoid smoking
- Lipid panel ordered (fasting)

Secondary Malignancy Screening:
- Allogeneic SCT survivors, particularly those with cGVHD, are at elevated risk for secondary solid tumors (including squamous cell carcinoma of the skin, oropharynx, esophagus, and others) and secondary MDS/AML (though the latter is less applicable given donor hematopoiesis)
- Annual full-body skin examination by dermatology
- Routine age-appropriate cancer screening: cervical cancer (Pap smear/HPV), breast cancer (mammography), colorectal cancer (colonoscopy at age 50 or sooner given GI cGVHD history)
- Patient educated regarding sun protection (UV exposure can trigger GVHD and increases skin cancer risk)

Vaccination:
- Live vaccines remain deferred until at least 2 years post-transplant and confirmed absence of immunosuppression
- Inactivated vaccines scheduled: seasonal influenza (annual), COVID-19 booster, pneumococcal (PCV15/PPSV23 per post-transplant schedule), meningococcal, Tdap, recombinant zoster vaccine (Shingrix) -- schedule per ASTCT/IDSA post-transplant immunization guidelines, starting at 6 months post-transplant for most killed vaccines (patient has been receiving these)
- HPV vaccine series if not previously received (to be confirmed)

Endocrine:
- Annual metabolic panel to screen for post-transplant diabetes mellitus (tacrolimus exposure)
- Annual TSH (noted above)
- DEXA scan for bone density (ordered)

Fertility/Gonadal Function:
- Myeloablative busulfan-containing conditioning results in infertility; patient previously counseled on this. Confirm current understanding and address questions.
- Refer to gynecology/reproductive endocrinology for evaluation of ovarian function and management of premature ovarian insufficiency (POI), which is expected in this setting -- estrogen replacement therapy discussion

Quality of Life / Psychosocial:
- Patient reports good overall QoL; some anxiety regarding relapse, which is understandable and appropriate
- Discussed the significance of today's negative bone marrow findings in the context of prognosis
- Referral to survivorship counseling and/or cancer psychology support services offered; patient will consider
- Encouraging return to full-time teaching and normal activities as tolerated
- American Cancer Society survivorship resources provided

6. MEDICATIONS AT THIS VISIT

- Levothyroxine 75 mcg PO daily (pre-existing hypothyroidism)
- Daily low-dose aspirin 81 mg (cardiovascular prophylaxis, per patient report)
- Preservative-free artificial tears PRN (new -- for dry eyes)
- All post-transplant prophylactic antimicrobials (letermovir, TMP-SMX, fluconazole, acyclovir) were discontinued per protocol by day +365
- Tacrolimus discontinued at month 11
- No active GVHD-directed therapy

7. PLAN SUMMARY AND FOLLOW-UP

- Next clinic visit in 3 months (routine surveillance); at that time: CBC, CMP, peripheral blood chimerism, and clinical assessment
- Bone marrow biopsy planned at 18 months post-transplant unless clinical indication arises earlier
- Ophthalmology referral placed today
- DEXA scan ordered today
- Echocardiogram ordered (late effects surveillance)
- Fasting lipid panel ordered
- Gynecology referral placed for POI management
- Dermatology appointment scheduled for full-body skin check
- Patient given detailed written survivorship care plan
- Emergency contact numbers provided; instructed to return or call immediately with any fever >100.4 degrees F, new bruising/bleeding, significant fatigue, or other concerning symptoms

Patient verbalized understanding of today's discussion and plan. Questions answered. She expressed gratitude for the care team and optimism regarding her future. She appeared emotionally well and appropriately motivated for long-term follow-up.

Time spent: approximately 60 minutes, of which greater than 50% devoted to counseling and coordination of care.

Electronically signed,
Attending Physician, Hematology/Oncology and Bone Marrow Transplant
